installed_capacity.note: Discrepancy between sources — likely different counting methodology (gross vs net, connected vs commissioned). Use 28–32 GW range.

residential_market.note: Government subsidy for 3–10 kW residential systems. Local (city/county) supplements available. Budget exhausted by May each year (2024: May)

rps_system.rec_spot_market_status: under review — potential phase-out due to price volatility

key_events: May 30 2024: KEPCO designated 103 substations in Gwangju/Jeonnam, 61 in Jeonbuk, 25 East Coast, 16 Jeju as 'system management substations' with saturated access capacity
April 2025: Korea MOTIE fined 8 solar operators and cancelled 11 projects totaling 347.4 MW for violating output curtailment orders (Jeollanam province)
2040 projection: up to 32% of solar/wind curtailed as VRE exceeds 20% penetration (Energy power model)
grid_curtailment_crisis.compensation_framework: None — generators bear all curtailment losses. No systematic compensation system exists.
grid_curtailment_crisis.china_parallel: IDENTICAL to China's 2023-2024 grid restriction zones where 20+ provinces suspended or limited new distributed solar connections
grid_curtailment_crisis.source: IEEFA June 2025, PV Magazine April 2025, S&P Global March 2025

kepco_distributed_solar_plans.source: PV Magazine May 2026, PV Magazine February 2026

monitoring_saas_landscape.note: WebSearch returned ZERO Korean domestic solar monitoring SaaS startups. Global platforms (SOLARMAN, iSolarCloud) serve Korean installers but no Korean-specific monitoring platform competitor identified. This is an analytically significant gap vs China's rich monitoring SaaS ecosystem.
monitoring_saas_landscape.implication: Korean monitoring SaaS market appears underdeveloped — opportunity for Chinese platforms (white-label) or Korean startup entry

solar_leasing_market.note: No Korean equivalent of Huaxia Financial Leasing found. Current Korean model is government subsidy (50% of installation cost) + private loan, not pure lease. The 'zero-capex rooftop' model does not appear to exist in Korea as of May 2026.
solar_leasing_market.opportunity: First-mover opportunity for a Korean bank subsidiary or state-backed finance vehicle to launch residential solar leasing product, analogous to Huaxia's 2015 launch

structural_comparison_china_korea.grid_curtailment: CONVERGENT — both China (2023-24) and Korea (2024-25) face identical grid saturation crisis; Korea designated 205+ system-management substations in May 2024
structural_comparison_china_korea.financing_model: DIVERGENT — China developed institutional leasing at scale; Korea relies on government subsidy + private loan; no Korean Huaxia equivalent exists
structural_comparison_china_korea.monitoring_saas: DIVERGENT — China has rich competitive monitoring SaaS ecosystem; Korea is early-stage
structural_comparison_china_korea.installation_market: DIVERGENT — China's residential market is consolidating at 50+ GW/year scale; Korea at 2.5 GW/year residential additions is 20x smaller
structural_comparison_china_korea.policy_direction: CONVERGENT — both China and Korea are shifting from FIT/subsidy to market-based compensation; both face grid integration challenges at scale
structural_comparison_china_korea.consumer_electronics_entry: UNEXPLORED in Korea — Hyundai, Samsung, LG have not entered residential solar the way Skyworth/TCL did in China
